latest.title: Board change
latest.content:
Today 07:00
RNS Number : 4836K
Genedrive PLC
01 July 2026
1 July 2026
genedrive plc
("genedrive" or the "Company")
Board change
genedrive plc (AIM: GDR), the point of care pharmacogenetic testing company, today announces that further to the Company's announcement of 26 May 2026 detailing various immediate and proposed board changes, Dr. Ian Gilham, Chairman and a Director of genedrive, has today stepped down from the Board.
The recruitment process for a new independent Non-Executive Chairman as well as two other independent Non-Executive Directors is progressing and further announcements will be made in due course.
As also confirmed in the 26 May 2026 announcement, Chris Yates continues to act as an independent Non-Executive Director pending the appointment of new Non-Executive Board members.
Gino Miele, CEO of genedrive plc, said:
"I would like to thank Ian for his commitment to genedrive and for the invaluable contributions he has made over many years in helping to shape the Company and its mission to transform patient outcomes through rapid pharmacogenetic testing. On behalf of the Board and the entire team at genedrive, both past and present, I wish him every success for the future. We look forward to welcoming our new Non-Executive Chairman and Non-Executive Directors and will update the market in due course."
genedrive plc

About genedrive plc (
http://www.genedrive.com
).
genedrive plc is a UK-based, commercial-stage pharmacogenetic testing company focused on rapid, point-of-care diagnostic tests to guide safe and effective drug prescription in emergency and acute care settings.
genedrive's proprietary technology platform enables clinicians to prescribe safer and more effective therapies based on a patient's genetic profile, supporting improved outcomes while reducing pressure on healthcare systems, lowering downstream healthcare costs through the prevention of avoidable complications and earlier, more effective intervention
The Company has two CE-IVD approved and NICE-recommended tests in NHS clinical use. The Genedrive® CYP2C19 ID Kit identifies stroke patients who will not respond to the current standard of care, Clopidogrel, used to reduce risk of secondary stroke. The Genedrive® MT-RNR1 ID Kit helps prevent antibiotic-induced hearing loss (AIHL) in newborns, enabling treatment decisions within actionable timeframes at the point of care.
Headquartered in Manchester, genedrive is focused on scaling UK-developed precision diagnostics within routine care and leveraging real-world evidence to support broader international adoption and commercial growth.

latest.title: CYP2C19 Agreement with Thermo Fisher Scientific
latest.content:
Today 07:00
RNS Number : 0755K
Genedrive PLC
29 June 2026
29 June 2026
genedrive plc
("genedrive" or the "Company")
CYP2C19 Product Development Collaboration agreement with Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.
genedrive plc (AIM: GDR), the point-of-care pharmacogenetic testing company, is pleased to announce a collaboration agreement with Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. enabling genedrive to develop a CYP2C19 pharmacogenetic in vitro diagnostic ("IVD") test for deployment on Thermo Fisher Scientific's QuantStudio™ 5 Dx real-time PCR laboratory platform (the Genedrive® CYP2C19 HT Kit).
The Genedrive® CYP2C19 HT Kit is designed for centralised, high-throughput laboratory testing, addressing a different market segment to the Company's rapid Genedrive® CYP2C19 ID Kit, which targets near-patient settings. Together, the two products position genedrive to serve both decentralised and laboratory-based CYP2C19 pharmacogenetic testing pathways. The Genedrive® CYP2C19 HT Kit is on track for UKCA certification around the end of the 2026 calendar year, initially targeting the UK NHS genomic laboratory market.
The Genedrive® CYP2C19 HT kit targets the same clinically relevant allele panel (*2, *3, *4, *8, *17 and *35) to maximise ethnic inclusivity and is configured as a multiplexed PCR reaction, enabling high sample throughput per run. It is supplied in a lyophilised, ambient temperature-stable format and operates directly from blood without nucleic acid extraction, reducing workflow complexity, hands-on time, and consumable use in high-throughput laboratory environments, maximising sample throughput and minimising hands-on time, processing steps, and consumable use.
There are approximately 100,000 new stroke patients annually in the UK, with approximately 94 million people living with the effects of stroke globally, at a cost of $890 billion per year. In the US each year, there are approximately 690,000 ischaemic strokes, 240,000 TIAs, and 550,000 percutaneous coronary interventions ("PCI") in coronary artery disease.
CYP2C19 pharmacogenetic testing is recommended by the UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence ("NICE") (
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/htg724
) to guide the use of the antiplatelet drug clopidogrel following ischaemic stroke ("IS") or transient ischaemic attack ("TIA"). NICE recommends laboratory-based CYP2C19 genotype testing and the use of the Genedrive® CYP2C19 ID Kit for rapid point-of-care testing when laboratory-based testing is not available. The American Heart Association supports CYP2C19 genotyping to guide antiplatelet therapies in coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, and stroke, and CYP2C19 genotyping.
Dr Gino Miele, CEO of genedrive plc, said:
"This collaboration expands our CYP2C19 offering into the high-throughput laboratory diagnostics market, complementing our established point-of-care solution. In addition to access to industry standard chemistries, this collaboration with Thermo Fisher Scientific will enable us to leverage a large global installed base of laboratory diagnostic PCR platforms, accelerating access to a significant new market segment while strengthening our existing pharmacogenetic testing portfolio."

latest.title: Manchester NHS Trust transitions MT-RNR1 to BAU
latest.content:
Today 07:00
RNS Number : 7377I
Genedrive PLC
18 June 2026
18 June 2026
genedrive plc
("genedrive" or the "Company")
Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust transitions genedrive's MT-RNR1 test to Business as Usual clinical service
genedrive plc (AIM: GDR), the point of care pharmacogenetic testing company, is pleased to announce that Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust ("MFT") hospitals has transitioned the Genedrive® MT-RNR1 ID kit for the prevention of Antibiotic Induced Hearing Loss in neonates to Business as Usual ("BAU") routine clinical service.
MFT hospitals (St. Mary's, North Manchester and Wythenshawe) together represent approximately 1,800 Neonatal Intensive Care Units ("NICU") admissions annually and have been three of the eight Greater Manchester sites participating in the 14-site nationwide PALOH-UK programme, which aims to address National Institute for Clinical Care and Excellence ("NICE") Real World Evidence generation requirements. To date, more than 30 critically ill babies in Greater Manchester NICUs have been identified with the MT-RNR1 variant, enabling alternative antibiotic prescription and reducing the risk of aminoglycoside-induced lifelong hearing loss. At least one baby carrying the MT-RNR1 variant has been identified at each NICU site in Greater Manchester, reinforcing the case for routine testing across neonatal services.
Dr Ajit Mahaveer, Consultant Neonatologist, Saint Mary's Hospital, Manchester said
:
"The Genedrive assay has become an integral part of our neonatal practice, enabling rapid identification of babies at risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss. Its continued use supports safer, more personalised care and allows timely treatment decisions without delaying antibiotic administration."
Dr Gino Miele, CEO of genedrive plc, said:
"The transition to BAU routine clinical service following PALOH-UK at MFT is a key milestone in driving wider scale adoption of our MT-RNR1 genetic test in the UK. As a Manchester-based company working closely with NHS partners, we are particularly proud that MFT has been central to the development and progression of rapid pharmacogenetic testing in NICUs. Since the introduction of the test, more than 30 babies in Greater Manchester alone have been identified as carrying the MT-RNR1 variant and would otherwise have been at risk of lifelong hearing loss. We are delighted that babies and families served by MFT will continue to benefit from the routine availability of our test.
"We are in active discussions with other hospitals in the PALOH-UK programme to support the transition of our MT-RNR1 test from PALOH-UK based funding to embedding into routine practice to ensure that newborn babies across the UK continue to have access to this preventive test."
Funding for PALOH-UK sites is provided by the Office for Life Sciences("OLS") and National Institute for Health and Care Research ("NIHR") and will cease by June 2026, with some sites completing earlier. Subsequent review and issuance of final guidance by NICE is expected around July 2027.

company_30s.what_they_do: GDR is a biotech company that builds diagnostic kits to test how patients metabolise drugs and to detect mitochondrial DNA mutations. Their main products are a CYP2C19 enzyme‑testing kit for personalised medicine and an MT‑RNR1 kit used by the NHS to guide treatment for hearing loss and other conditions.
company_30s.why_they_matter: Personalised medicine is a huge, growing market. The CYP2C19 test helps doctors choose the right dose of common drugs like clopidogrel, reducing side‑effects and improving outcomes. The MT‑RNR1 kit is already being used by NHS trusts to streamline care. Both products sit at the intersection of genetics, pharmacology and healthcare cost‑saving, giving the company a clear niche.
company_30s.current_state: GDR is still in the early‑stage, pre‑revenue phase. It has secured a partnership with Thermo Fisher, received NHS pilots, and earned an R&D tax credit. The board has recently changed, signalling a possible shift in strategy. The stock is trading at a very low price, suggesting it is still far below any realistic valuation.

rns_narrative: GDR has moved from research to partnership. The Thermo Fisher deal will provide access to a global distribution network and validate the product’s technical quality.
The NHS trusts are beginning to use the MT‑RNR1 kit, giving the company a real‑world use case and a potential revenue stream once NICE recommends it.
The board shuffle and the R&D tax credit signal that management is tightening the focus on regulatory milestones and cash‑flow optimisation.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Waiting for regulatory green lights to unlock a revenue stream
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bulls are pointing to the upcoming FDA 510(k) submission and the NHS pilot as catalysts that could finally bring the company into the market.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears worry that the company still has to clear several regulatory hurdles and that the NHS pilot may not translate into large‑scale adoption. They also note the board changes as a sign of instability.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The chatter is quiet but focused on the next regulatory milestone. When that announcement drops, the community will likely shift from cautious optimism to a buying push.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat – the public hasn’t been talking about this company. That means any news will have a high surprise factor.

near_term_catalysts: FDA 510(k) submission for the CYP2C19 kit – a formal approval would allow commercial sales in the US.
NICE recommendation for the MT‑RNR1 kit – would open up NHS procurement and create a large, predictable revenue source.
A public announcement of a new partnership or funding round that provides working capital for scale‑up.
A spike in trading volume that matches a news release, indicating market acceptance.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a clear FDA approval announcement or a NICE recommendation. Pay attention to any sudden volume increase and a price move above the recent high of the 52‑week range.
expect_next.timing_regime: If the share is still deeply discounted (70‑95% off its peak) and volume has dried up, the stock is likely in the early stage of a mean‑reversion play. A breakout from a consolidation zone or a jump to the 52‑week high would be the confirmation to start buying.

buy_vs_sell.lean: Speculative Long – the company is still too early for a full contrarian bull case but there is upside if regulatory milestones hit.
buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If FDA and NICE approvals come through, the company could start generating revenue and the market will likely re‑price the shares. A 70% discount to a future valuation of, say, £2 per share would give a 200% upside if the price moves to £6. The asymmetry is high because the downside is capped by the current low price.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: Regulatory delays or rejection would stall revenue. The company still has no cash flow and could run out of runway if it doesn’t secure funding. A worst‑case scenario is a further 20‑30% drop if the market loses confidence.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential loss: up to the current price (~£0.00‑£0.10). Potential gain: up to several pounds if the company lands the approvals. The risk/reward ratio is roughly 1:10 or better if the company succeeds.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter after a clear breakout above the recent 52‑week high, using a stop just below the prior support level. Size the position so that a 10% drop would wipe out the trade, but a 30% gain would double the investment. Use a trailing stop to lock in profits.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash or a small position until the FDA or NICE announcement. If volume spikes above 1.5× the 20‑day average on that day, consider a quick entry.
playbook.if_passing: If the company fails to obtain FDA approval or receives a negative NICE recommendation, consider exiting. No evidence of delisting or bankruptcy, so this risk is low.

ai_insights.bottom_line: GDR is a pre‑revenue biotech on the cusp of regulatory approvals; if it clears FDA and NICE, the steep discount could deliver a big upside, but the risk is that the company may never reach marketable revenue.
